speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been having these weird heart flutters—palpitations—since yesterday morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your vitals. Your diastolic blood pressure today is 78 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs and clinical presentation, the primary diagnosis is hypothyroidism.

speaker: doctor
text: I notice your temperature is elevated—39.9 °C. That’s concerning and not typical for uncomplicated hypothyroidism. Any chills or sore throat?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve had a low-grade fever off and on, but it spiked this morning—I felt really hot and shaky.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s check your blood glucose—it’s important given your symptoms and thyroid status.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose is 11.1 mmol/L—elevated, especially fasting. Have you noticed increased thirst or urination?

speaker: patient
text: Actually, yes—I’ve been drinking more water and waking up twice at night to pee.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 79 bpm—normal for now—but that contrasts with your palpitations. We’ll need an ECG shortly.

speaker: patient
text: I’m also feeling fatigued and my skin has been dry, even though I’ve been using moisturizer daily.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic hypothyroid symptoms. But the fever and hyperglycemia suggest something else may be going on—possibly thyroiditis or an intercurrent infection.

speaker: patient
text: I did have a mild cough last week, but it went away. Could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly. Let’s order a TSH, free T4, CRP, and blood cultures to rule out subacute thyroiditis or sepsis.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking levothyroxine 50 mcg daily since my last visit—should I keep taking it?

speaker: doctor
text: Given the fever and possible inflammation, I’m going to hold levothyroxine temporarily while we investigate further.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. Also, my home BP monitor showed something different earlier—I think around 63 for the bottom number?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s interesting—we just recorded 78 mmHg diastolic earlier, but let me recheck manually.

speaker: doctor
text: This reading shows 63 mmHg diastolic—lower than before. That drop could reflect dehydration or early septic physiology, especially with the fever and high glucose.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked again just now, it was back to 78 mmHg—the same as that reading from earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: Good—consistency helps. We’ll repeat vitals hourly and start IV fluids while we await labs. Let’s also add metformin 500 mg once daily pending endocrine review, given your persistent hyperglycemia.
